So as you can see, if you just type in the keyword, right, so let's just say the term investing my money over age 50. Let's just take that as an example here, okay? So this phrase is a broad match, meaning that what's going to happen by default, is Google is also going to display your ads for any terms that they think are similar to this, just very broad. So, if people say, how to invest when I'm middle-aged, or whatever it might be that they associate with this term or they think is similar, then they're going to display it, okay? Now there's another option which is called a phrase match, okay? So for example, if we put these little quotations, and this is your cheat sheet down here, keyword and quotations equals phrase match. Then what that means is generally speaking, Google is only going to show your ad. If the search term that's typed in includes the term investing my money over the age of 50. Now they do take a few liberties, so if somebody typed in how to invest my money over age 50, they're probably going to display it for that. But what that means is that anything that contains that phrase for the most part, right? So if somebody says investing my money over the age of 50, all right? Let's just say that they typed in, investing my money over the age of 50, if I live in the state of Pennsylvania, then it's probably not going to get shown, okay? So it's really looking for things that contain this phrase right here. That's why they call it a phrase match. And there's a third level called an exact match. So if you're like, I only want this to go to people who type in investing my money over age 50, and that's it, then you put these brackets right here around there, the little square brackets, and it's only going to show to those people.
